mappings.rationaleAI: Diplomacy is the primary instrument of C (foreign policy and diplomacy), covering C.1 (aims of foreign policy), C.2 (use of diplomacy in negotiation, arbitration, mediation, public international conferences, and secret diplomacy) with comprehensive historical and structural coverage.

NATO is a paradigm case for A.2 (international organizations), B.1 (collective security treaties and nonaggression obligations under Article 5), and E.3 (organized military forces designed for defense across ground, naval, and air branches).

War comprehensively covers section E: types of warfare (E.1 — conventional, civil, guerrilla, total, nuclear), conduct of war including strategy and tactics (E.2.a, E.2.b), causes, effects, and ethics including international humanitarian law (E.2.e).

mappings.articleTitle: International Red Cross and Red Crescent Movement
mappings.rationaleAI: The ICRC and IFRC are the defining non-governmental organizations for A.2 (nonnational organizations and agencies) and directly implements E.2.e (international law relating to the treatment of persons during wartime) through the Geneva Conventions.

Human rights treaties (UDHR, regional regimes) are central to B (international treaties and agreements), C.1 (compatibility of foreign policy with humanitarian interests), and E.2.e (treatment of persons during wartime under international law).

The UN is the central institution of A.2 (international organizations), B.1 (collective security via Security Council), C.3 (foreign aid and economic/cultural/technical organizations), and E.2.e (international humanitarian law and peacekeeping mandates).

Imperialism covers A.1 (transnational ideological movements like anti-imperialism and Pan-nationalism), C.1 (how imperial foreign policy pursues hegemony versus humanitarian interests), and E (military conquest as core mechanism of imperial expansion).

The Cold War covers A.1 (transnational ideological blocs), B.1–3 (collective security treaties, arms limitation agreements), C (foreign policy competition and détente diplomacy), D (espionage and counterintelligence as the defining covert dimension), and E.1 (proxy wars and limited warfare as the primary conflict mode).

Military comprehensively covers E.2 (conduct of war: strategy, tactics, logistics, budget management) and E.3 (armed forces: types of soldiers, branches including ground, naval, and air forces, and auxiliary corps including intelligence and logistics).

Terrorism covers E.1's category of political and irregular warfare against non-combatants, and D's counterterrorism intelligence activities as the primary state response to non-state political violence in the conduct of international affairs and national security.

Colonialism covers A.1 (transnational political movements including anti-colonialism and Pan-Africanism), C.1 (colonial foreign policy aims and their incompatibility with humanitarian interests), and E (military conquest as the mechanism of colonial expansion and resistance).

Decolonization covers A.1 (Pan-Africanism, Pan-Arabism, and other transnational independence movements), B.2 (treaties terminating colonial arrangements), and C (foreign aid and US/UN policy supporting independence movements in the post-WWII international order).

The EU is a paradigm case for A.2 (international organizations) and directly covers the priority path B.5 (economic and commercial treaties) through its single market, customs union, and common trade policy as foundational treaty-based economic integration.

Peace covers B.1 (collective security and nonaggression frameworks), C.1 (foreign policy compatibility with humanitarian interests), and C.2 (diplomacy, arbitration, and mediation as the primary instruments of international dispute resolution and peace maintenance).

Nationalism is the ideological foundation of A.1's transnational political movements — anti-colonial nationalism, Pan-Slavism, Pan-Arabism, and ethnic nationalisms all fall within the category of transnational political movements shaping international relations.

The Scramble for Africa covers B.4 (territorial treaties, the Berlin Conference's partitioning of Africa as territorial agreements) and E (military conquest and African resistance movements as the armed dimension of imperial expansion).

Washington's career covers C.1 (his Farewell Address articulating foundational US foreign policy principles of neutrality) and E.3.a (his role leading a volunteer army of colonial forces — a paradigm case of non-professional military forces).

Fortifications are a core element of military strategy (E.2.a) and tactics (E.2.b), and represent the establishment and maintenance of defensive military infrastructure (E.3.c — ground forces' defensive infrastructure).

The Atlantic slave trade operated through commercial treaties and inter-state economic agreements (B.5 — economic and commercial treaties), including bilateral arrangements among European powers regulating slave-trade routes and trading rights (asiento contracts).

Mao developed Maoist guerrilla warfare doctrine (E.1 — guerrilla warfare as a type of war) and the Long March military strategy (E.2.a — military strategy), which became foundational contributions to revolutionary military theory in international relations.

Bolívar's campaigns cover E.1 (national wars of independence and civil wars across six nations) and E.3.a (types of soldiers — volunteer and conscripted armies mobilized in continental liberation campaigns).

Russia as a permanent UN Security Council member covers A.2, its nuclear deterrence posture and arms treaties cover B.1 and B.3, Cold War and contemporary foreign policy covers C, and its intelligence/espionage operations (FSB, GRU) substantially address the priority path D.

The state is the primary actor in international relations, covering A (the politics of international relations between sovereign entities), C.1 (foreign policy aims grounded in state interests), and E (war as organized interstate violence).

Transport directly covers the priority path B.6 (communications and transportation treaties), as international transport systems — aviation agreements, maritime conventions, rail and road access treaties — are foundational to the international treaty framework.

News covers B.7 (cultural exchange treaties, as international media and information flow are regulated through cultural exchange frameworks) and D (state control of information as an instrument of national security and intelligence, including propaganda and public diplomacy).

The British Empire covers B.4 (territorial treaties and colonial land transfers), B.5 (mercantile and commercial treaties), C (British foreign policy and imperial diplomacy), and E (military conquest and naval hegemony as instruments of imperial expansion).

The Enlightenment fostered transnational intellectual exchange (B.7 — cultural exchange) through salons, encyclopedias, and correspondence networks, and shaped C.1 through its development of natural rights theory and the compatibility of foreign policy with humanitarian interests.

Suleiman's reign covers B.2 (Ottoman treaties terminating wars and fixing borders with Safavids and European powers) and E/E.2.a (Ottoman military campaigns and siege warfare strategy from Vienna to the eastern Mediterranean).
